column1: What Java EE mechanism allows objects to obtain references to resources without direct instantiation?
What is the primary architectural benefit of using injection in Java EE?
column2: It decouples code from the implementations of its dependencies.

column1: How does injection simplify developer code in Java EE?
column2: It removes the need for manual instantiation of dependencies.

column1: What are the two specific injection mechanisms provided by the Java EE platform?
How are injection points marked within a Java class?
column2: By decorating fields or methods with specific injection annotations.

column1: When does the container provide required instances to marked injection points?
What specific injection type is used to access resources in the JNDI namespace?
Name three examples of container-managed objects that can receive resource injection.
column2: Servlets, enterprise beans, and managed beans.

column1: What are common examples of resources injected via the JNDI namespace?
column2: Data sources, connectors, or custom resources.

column1: Why is an interface typically used as the reference type for an injected resource?
column2: To decouple the code from the specific implementation of the resource.

column1: Which annotation is primarily used for resource injection?
What is the injection style called when an annotation is placed directly on a class member variable?
What is the injection style called when an annotation is placed on a setter method?
What naming convention must a method follow to be valid for method-based injection?
column2: JavaBeans conventions (the name must begin with 'set').

column1: What is the required return type for a method used in method-based injection?
How many parameters are allowed in a method used for resource injection?
In which package is the @Resource annotation located?
Which JSR defines the Common Annotations for the Java Platform, including @Resource?
On what basis does resource injection resolve the object to be injected?
Why is resource injection considered not typesafe?
column2: The object type is not known at compile time.

column1: What is a common consequence of type mismatch in resource injection?
column2: The application will encounter runtime errors.

column1: What mechanism allows regular Java classes to be turned into managed objects and injected?
Who manages the lifecycle of instances provided via dependency injection?
What concept in dependency injection determines the lifecycle of container-instantiated objects?
What scope is appropriate for a managed object that only needs to exist for a single client request?
What scope is appropriate for a managed object that must process multiple requests within a session?
What is another term used for managed objects in the context of dependency injection?
How is a regular class defined as a managed bean?
column2: By assigning a scope to the class.

column1: Which annotation is used specifically to inject managed beans?
In which package is the @Inject annotation located?
On what basis does dependency injection resolve the object to be injected?
Why is dependency injection considered typesafe?
column2: It resolves dependencies based on their Java type.

column1: How can you decouple code from a managed bean's implementation when using DI?
column2: Reference the injected instance using an interface type.

column1: Which JSR defines Contexts and Dependency Injection (CDI) for the Java EE Platform?
Can resource injection be used to inject regular classes directly?
Can dependency injection be used to inject JNDI resources directly?
Which injection mechanism allows for the direct injection of JNDI resources?
Which injection mechanism allows for the direct injection of regular Java classes?
The @Resource(name="java:comp/DefaultDataSource") annotation targets which specific resource in GlassFish?
The statement 'dependency injection is typesafe because it resolves by type' is an example of _____ knowledge.
Which mechanism identifies dependencies by their JNDI resource name?
What is the primary difference in how Resource Injection and DI resolve dependencies?
column2: Resource injection resolves by name, while DI resolves by type.

column1: Is it possible to use method-based injection with @Resource?
What is the effect of applying @javax.enterprise.context.RequestScoped to a class?
column2: It turns the class into a managed object with a request-based lifecycle.

column1: Which injection mechanism is prone to runtime errors if types do not match?
What is the standard name for the JSR covering Common Annotations for the Java Platform?
True or False: Dependency injection allows the container to manage the lifecycle of injected instances.
True or False: Resource injection is typesafe.
True or False: In method-based injection, the setter must have a void return type.
True or False: Dependency injection resolves objects by name.

The _____ annotation enables the injection of any resource available in the JNDI namespace.
Managed objects used in DI are also known as _____.
Method-based injection requires setter methods to follow _____ conventions.
In the example `@Resource(name="java:comp/DefaultDataSource") private DataSource dsc;`, what is the injection point?
What determines how long a managed object instantiated by the container remains in memory?
Does resource injection support injecting custom resources available in JNDI?
Which injection mechanism is described in Chapter 23 of the Java EE documentation?
What is the consequence of not using interfaces for injected references?
column2: The code becomes more tightly coupled to specific implementations.
